summary: Gruber examines the peculiar split in how U.S. companies are handling TikTok's post-ban status: cloud providers Oracle and Akamai restored service on Trump's word, while Apple and Google continue to keep ByteDance apps out of their app stores. He theorizes this isn't coincidental but rather a deliberate strategy to slowly squeeze TikTok rather than kill it overnight, turning pressure on ByteDance to sell rather than on the U.S. government to reverse the ban. The instant ban created backlash from users directed at politicians, but the current arrangement — existing users can keep using TikTok but can't reinstall it or get updates — creates a slow decline that pressures ByteDance instead. Gruber questions what happens if China simply waits it out, noting the CCP has no financial pressure to capitulate and doesn't lack patience.
key_points: ByteDance apps remain legally banned in the U.S. — TikTok only works because cloud providers restored service on Trump's informal assurance, not because the law changed
The split between cloud providers (restoring service) and app stores (maintaining the ban) creates a slow squeeze: no new users, no updates, and no reinstallation on new devices
The instant ban backfired by directing user outrage at the U.S. government rather than at ByteDance, so the current half-measure better targets pressure on the Chinese side
The CCP faces no financial pressure to allow a sale and has the patience to simply wait out the situation, which could eventually lead to TikTok users refusing to upgrade their phones
Apple has a significant commercial interest in this standoff because users avoiding phone upgrades to preserve TikTok access directly hurts iPhone sales
money_quotes: TikTok returning to operational service in the U.S. after an outage of just one day has squelched the outrage surrounding its ban.
Nobody is really talking about the fact that ByteDance apps remain banned, legally speaking, in the U.S. right now.
With TikTok's cloud provider access shut off, it was like a guillotine. TikTok was working for everyone in the U.S., then boom, it was shut down for everyone.
Being locked out of the App Store and Play Store, on the other hand, is a slow squeeze.
Wipe and restore your phone from backup? No more TikTok. Buy a new phone? No TikTok on the new phone.

Instantly banning TikTok's US operations resulted in instant and vociferous outrage from TikTok users — the pressure turned out not to be on ByteDance and the CCP, it was on the US government to give people back their beloved TikTok.
Slowly strangle TikTok over a period of months, rather than behead it overnight.
Say what you will about the Chinese government, but they do not lack for patience.
key_insight: The current TikTok arrangement — functional for existing users but absent from app stores — may be a deliberate strategy to slowly pressure ByteDance into selling rather than triggering the immediate user backlash that an outright ban created.
